channel.title: Comments for page "Salt and Pepper (Extra Salty)"
channel.description: Posts in the discussion thread "Salt and Pepper (Extra Salty)"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yes, I've come to really like these characters. Really makes me want to read more of them! +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: … I want to give Wren a hug. Or, if they don't like hugs, a plushie I've charged with hugs so they can control the touch.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: reading this series of stories is what made me want to join the site. great job, all of the stories are very enjoyable.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Of all the stories in this series, this has got to be my favorite. In addition to A Random Day's comments, I would also like to add that this story was amazingly understandable. While I don't have a very firm grasp on a lot of the concepts presented in this piece, the language and framing (plus context) made sure that I was possessed with the ghosts of those concepts for at least the duration of the story. Special props to how you dealt with pataphysics.

+1!

The Shuttered Eye

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thank you kindly! I love writing these characters — they've become much more distinct in my mind since the one-off jokes of SCP-4581. I'm glad the central gimmick comes across well, too; I feel like simple referentialism is too easy, so I try to make it a transformative thing. Looking forward to sharing more!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This does an excellent job conveying a metric ton of exposition without being boring. Penelope and Wren are entertaining characters that feel grounded in a contemporary fiction setting, and the incorporation of the Salt and Pepper Diner skit into the narrative is perfectly tuned for a Gamers Against Weed story without inducing eye-rolls. I look forward to the continuing adventures of Steak and Book.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
alexa they are so angry play track 6 forever

This installment is (obviously) inspired by John Mulaney's excellent "Salt and Pepper Diner" routine. As it turns out, the joke is not so fun for the captive audience — particularly when it's being used as pataphysical camouflage.

Thanks go out to Zyn for providing draft critique, and to kinchtheknifeblade for looking over this story pre-release! Much appreciated!

Easter Eggs
Easter Eggs
  • "The Incredible Melting Man" (1977) was featured in Episode 704 of MST3K. (Wren isn't quite "genre-savvy" enough to realize that their choice of attire might foreshadow an emotional meltdown.)
  • Gore's scheme reminds Masterson of John le Carré's "The Spy Who Came In from the Cold" (1963), which explores the relationship between personal motivation and amorality through a series of elaborate feints.
  • Masterson compares their "tracking" issues to Christopher Nolan's "Memento" (2000), which is a mystery/drama (presented mostly in reverse order) about an amnesiac who marks their experiences with photos and tattoos. This also dovetails with their circumvention of Foundation amnestics: they have a Handycam magically wired to their optic nerve. Just in case.
  • Wren quotes Alan Moore and David Lloyd's "V for Vendetta" (1982) on their way out the door. Partially because of the obvious anarchist influence, and partially because Anna Bojarski spent way too much time on 4chan before the exhibition in 2008.
  • "23" (2:30) continues to be meaningless. Probably. Mostly. Don't worry about it.
